The referendum involves a package of constitutional reforms outlined in the July National Charter, which includes 47 constitutional changes and 37 legal reforms aimed at enhancing accountability, balancing institutions, and introducing term limits, among other things[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2026_Bangladeshi_constitutional_referendum).

- (A) This suggests transitioning to a unicameral parliament from a bicameral one. However, Bangladesh's current parliament (Jatiya Sangsad) is already unicameral, and the reforms propose the opposite: introducing bicameralism[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2026_Bangladeshi_constitutional_referendum).
- (B) There's no mention of lifelong prime ministerial appointment with 90% support. Instead, the reforms impose term limits, stating that no person can serve as Prime Minister for more than 10 years[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2026_Bangladeshi_constitutional_referendum).
- (C) The reforms do not include any bans on religious parties like Jamaat-e-Islami or others[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2026_Bangladeshi_constitutional_referendum).
- (D) No proposals for relocating the capital from Dhaka to Khulna (or anywhere else) are part of the package[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2026_Bangladeshi_constitutional_referendum).
- (E) This matches directly: The reforms explicitly include creating a bicameral parliament by establishing a 100-member upper house, distributed proportionally based on party votes in the election[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2026_Bangladeshi_constitutional_referendum). This is a key structural change listed in the referendum provisions.

Thus, the correct change included in the reform package is the creation of an upper house of parliament.

<final_answer>
(E)
</final_answer>
